F1 0.7662 0.7686
The performance of the classification is evaluated using predicted values from the confusion matrix with the following metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, and f1-score. Table 4 shows the best performance metric results for the models.
The success of the model is not determined by the results. However, it should demonstrate functionality.
At the very least, the performance should surpass a random classifier. For that reason, the success criteria of the data mining goals was set on a minimum accuracy of 60%. Considering that there was no class imbalance, both the 1D-CNN and 2D-CNN achieve this goal.
In this medical context, minimizing the risk of missing seizures is important, which is why recall is prioritized over precision. In this regard, 1D-CNN performs slightly better with a recall value of 0.83
compared to 2D-CNN with a score of 0.81. However, falsely classifying many non-seizure EEG segments as seizures could lead to a lack of trust in the model, potentially impacting the future implementation of such technology. This is why a balance between recall and precision is necessary. The success criteria for the F1 score is set between 0.7 and 0.8, which both models achieve with a score of 0.76.
Circling back to the business objectives, it should again be stated that this research has no intention of directly achieving them besides carrying out research for improvement of patient care. It was mentioned that an implemented model that is highly accurate could, in the future, help to reduce the shortage of nurses in the PICU. Besides this objective, further research might uncover novel biomarkers for seizure detection. This research serves as a foundation for future research that hopefully brings Erasmus MC
closer to achieving these objectives.
27

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

sections.heading: 5 Conclusion
sections.text:
5 Conclusion
This research focused on the development of a deep neural network for the classification of epileptic seizures in EEG fragments. The process involved understanding the medical context and the EEG
data, preparing the data for modeling after considering modeling techniques, creating a test and model design, performing modeling, and evaluating its results. Three sub-questions were formulated to navigate and provide an answer to the following main question:
How can deep learning be applied to minimally processed EEG data for the detection of epileptic seizures in pediatric comatose patients?
5.1 Sub-question 1
SQ 1: Which deep learning model architecture is most suitable for identifying the relevant patterns and features in an EEG that are indicative of an epileptic seizure?
While there are plenty of valid options of deep learning architectures for this task, the literature review proved a strong preference for the usage of 2D and 1D convolutional neural networks for the detection of seizures in an EEG. Considering the preference of using minimally processed EEG data - as medical experts are interested in learning fromraw data patterns - this is a fitting approach.
To answer this sub-question more specifically, both 2D layers with one-dimensional input and 1D
layers can be used in the CNN design. The theoretical framework shows that 1D convolutional layers are typically used in combination with minimally processed EEG data. Although data is generally transformed to a visual representation of the EEG when using 2D convolutional layers, it is also possible to leave out this process by forcing the CNN to observe one dimension in its channel size. This prevents the possible loss of information when converting to a spectrogram.
Until more seizure data is available, supervised learning remains the right approach. Once temporal aspects become more relevant to include in model results, a recurrent neural network or CNN-RNN
combination may be more appropriate for further research. This could be the case when there is an interest in analyzing an EEG recording in its entirety, instead of separate fragments as was the case in this research.
28

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige
5.2 Sub-question 2
SQ 2: Which preprocessing techniques should be applied to the raw EEG data to make it trainable for the chosen model?
The preprocessing techniques applied to the data are dependent on the characteristics of the data, the task at hand and the chosen model technique. In the context of this research it was desirable to keep the data as raw as possible, since medical professionals also have to work with the rough and noisy data in the field. This is why limited techniques are applied to clean, construct and format the data.
Artifacts caused by electrical interference are always removed and a bandpass filter is applied to clean the data. Other artifacts, such as from nursing or other movement could still exist in the data but are not cleaned. There is less need for doing this as the data concerns comatose patients. A reference point needs to be provided to serve as a base point for the electrodes. In this research, missing data occurred once and was handled by imputing with the mean values of other electrodes. This was done because the model assumes consistent dimension across segments, but in practice physicians will have to do with the electrodes available. Seeing that the task at hand was to classify segments of an EEG, the recordings were epoched with a fixed length of ten seconds and reshaped by grouping and aggregating channels values. For the model to classify a whole segment as seizure or non-seizure, each epoch was labeled based on the most common value. To avoid class imbalance, an equal number of seizure and non-seizure epochs were selected for each recording.
5.3 Sub-question 3
SQ 3: How well does the chosen deep learning model classify EEG segments as seizure on non-seizure?
The results presented two types of convolutions neural networks: one with 1D layers and the other with
2D layers. These models performed similarly on the classification and its related metrics, although differences were observed in the learning curves and predicted probabilities. The CNN with 1D layers showed a more stable loss curve over epochs with less of a gap between the training and validation set, compared to the CNN with 2D layers. In terms of accuracy over epochs, the 2D-CNN model showed a more steady curve. A model with 2D layers shows more confidence in probabilities close to 0 and 1, as the model with 1D layers predicted more segments around the probability threshold (0.5). This was also observed from the AUC-ROC curve.
The 1D-CNN showed a preference for classifying segments as seizure, with a higher number of correct as well as false classifications compared to the 2D-CNN. On the other hand, this was the opposite case for the 2D-CNN, which showed a preference for classifying segments as non-seizure. It correctly classified more non-seizure segments, but this also resulted in more false non-seizure segments compared to the results from the 1D-CNN.
The performance metrics reflect these results, as can be seen from the precision and recall score.
1D-CNN performed better with a recall score of 0.83 compared to 0.81. On the contrary, 2D-CNN
has a better precision score of 0.73 compared to 1D-CNN's 0.71. There is a 0.2 difference in these performance metrics. With this trade-off, both F1 scores are 0.77. The overall accuracy of the 2D-CNN
was slightly better with 0.76, compared to 0.75.
29

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

sections.heading: 5.4 Main question
sections.text:
5.4 Main question
In evaluation of these results, it is important to link back to the business objectives and data mining goals. These also relate to the main research question. The business objectives and its corresponding success criteria were defined as:
A healthy population and excellent care through research and education
Improve PICU bed occupancy and nurse staffing
Discover new biomarkers for seizures in EEG
The success criteria for these objectives was not based on direct achievement. Instead, the results and development of the model contribute by setting a foundation for further research. This takes us back to the data mining goals:
Develop a functional deep learning classification model that can distinguish between EEG
fragment with seizure activity and non-seizure brain activity
This is determined to be successful if:
The model performs better than a random classifier with at least 60% accuracy on a balanced dataset
Prioritizes recall, with an F1 score between 0.7 and 0.8
Both models achieved an accuracy of at least 75% and an F1 score of 0.77. Although the results were very similar, 1D-CNN performed the best according to the success criteria set, since it has a higher recall score. From a medical perspective, minimizing the risk of missing seizures is most important.
In conclusion, this research focused on developing a deep learning model for the classification of seizure and non-seizure EEG segments in comatose pediatric patients. The data mining goals, along with
Erasmus MC's objective to improve patient care through research, are achieved. The sub-questions reveal the steps that should be taken to preprocess the data in a way to minimally modifies the data without affecting its true representation. This is done by filtering with a high-pass of 0.1 Hz, a low-pass of 70 Hz and a notch filter that select the frequencies of 50 Hz and Hz to remove electrical noise. Referencing should be applied and missing data in the form of left out channels should be addressed. In accordance with the data mining success criteria and the sub-question answers, the most suitable deep learning technique for this minimally processed EEG data of pediatric comatose patients is determined to be a convolutional neural network with 1D layers. Looking forward, this research lays the groundwork for further research and innovation at Erasmus MC. The implementation of a highly accurate model could potentially address the shortage of nurses in the PICU, and ongoing research may uncover novel biomarkers for seizure detection.
30

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

sections.heading: 6.1 Interpretation of Results
sections.text:
6.1 Interpretation of Results
The performance metrics achieve the objectives, but the results are not excellent. For the purpose of the research this is fine, as the models are not expected to be implemented but serve as groundwork for further research. None of the models trained made it past epoch 15 when using EarlyStopping.
The learning curves accompany this, as they show strong signs of overfitting past epoch 15 or earlier.
In comparison, a reviewed article that was used as a reference (Gramacki and Gramacki, 2022) trained models with 300 epochs. With complex data, it was assumed that the developed models would show signs of underfitting instead.
The distribution of predictions as probabilities are not shared in the literature that was reviewed, which does not allow for comparison. In the results of this research, a lot of predictions has probabilities around 0.5, which would suggest that the model is not extremely confident about the classification.
The research states that recall is prioritized from a medical perspective, in order to not risk missing seizures. However, the one of the business objectives mentions the improvement of nurse staffing. A
point could be made from a nursing perspective that precision is equally important to recall, if not more. False alarms for seizure detection might only increase the workload of the medical staff. It is a trade-off that must be made and the stakeholders are expected to have different priorities.
Another interesting point of view is that of the (possibly) unknown indicators of seizures. If such indicators in EEG data truly exist and can be learned using deep learning, evaluation through performance metrics and a confusion matrix might not make sense. After all, some segments may include indications of seizures that a neurologist was not able to identify. This could technically result in reported false positives and negatives, without these truly being false. It should be mentioned that the results of this research are certainly not expected to have captured undetected seizures, but this could be an interesting thought for future research. This is why adequate techniques and visualization techniques will be necessary to implement to interpret the black-box results of a deep learning model.
A way to interpret the results from this research is that, due to using a validation set, the size of the training set is not large enough. This causes overfitting and limits the ability to correctly classify. It could also be the case that one of the recordings with many epochs contributes much to the model weights, but is not representative of the other patients. This could introduce bias to the model. The results may still be acceptable, but only because the test design includes epochs from patients with a lot of seizure data across each subset. This would mean that the model does not generalize well.

sections.heading: 6.2 Generalizability
sections.text:
6.2 Generalizability
While a validation set was used in the test design, it is important to note that no specific cross-validation technique was applied. The data splitting process involved a random separation of the data into subsets for training, validation, and testing. The single split was done without further stratification or grouping. This decision was made due to the available epoch per subject and the dataset size.
When training a 2D-CNN with no validation set and only train-test sets, it scored better on accuracy and precision compared to the model that used validation data during the training process. However, recall worsened. This could be explained by the size of the dataset. It is possible that using a portion of the data for validation resulted in insufficient data for training and improving its accuracy.
31

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige
Using a cross-validation strategy for splitting the data ensures robustness and avoids data leakage.
In a medical context using a subject-wise split is a standard, as otherwise the model might learn patterns in epochs from specific recordings from the training and validation set and will apply the corresponding weights to the test set. This could introduce a form of data leakage. Ensuring there is no overlap in patients across subsets can be done with GroupKFold cross-validation. However, the data used in this research has an imbalanced number of seizure epochs across patients. To solve this imbalance, stratification needs to be applied in combination with nonoverlapping groups through
StratifiedGroupKFold. This means that the size of the dataset could be excessively reduced, since every patient will need an equal amount of epochs.
Another option could be to apply the LeaveOneGroupOut cross-validation technique. During each model iteration, one patient's data would be used for testing, while the data from all other patients is used for training. Considering that seizure characteristics are patient-specific, using the technique might help the model to capture that, while ensuring that it can generalize across patients. Again, the impact of varying epoch counts is that patients with more recordings contribute more data to the training process.

sections.heading: 6.3 Recommendations
sections.text:
6.3 Recommendations
Cross-validation
Those conducting further research are advised to consider the impact of cross-validation on generalizability. Many studies use record-wise cross-validation, which does not ensure that samples from the same patient are not present in both training and test folds. This can overestimate the performance of a classifier. According to Saeb et al. (2016) and Tougui et al. (2021), the only right form of cross-validation in this context is subject-wise, with StratifiedGroupKFold and LeaveOneGroupOut being the only valid techniques. At the same time, researchers should acknowledge that successful implementation of this will depend on the balance of classes and samples. In order to stratify these and obtain satisfactory model results, it might be necessary to implement data augmentation techniques that increase the number of seizure epochs in patients or to increase the amount of seizure data overall.
Data Augmentation
As mentioned in the theoretical framework, data augmentation steps can be taken to increase the available data. This needs to be approached carefully to avoid bias. Using sliding windows and downsampling non-seizure segments can help address a small dataset. If a large number of epochs need to be generated, overlapping windows do not introduce new information. Instead, it could be more useful to develop a model that can generate fragments on its own.
Synthetic Data
As mentioned, an interesting avenue of research could be to use modeling techniques to generate synthetic EEG data that simulates seizures. Rasheed et al., 2021 proposed a deep convolutional generative adversarial network to generate synthetic EEG data based on other recordings before detecting seizures using a CNN model. Being able to use synthetic EEG data for modeling would significantly reduce the shortage and the time-consuming process which is acquiring EEG signals.
Additionally, this would make predictions easier to make when privacy concerns are less presented.
32

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

sections.heading: 6.4 Process Review
sections.text:
6.4 Process Review
Working with the CRISP-DM methodology resulted in multiple iterations of phases in this project.
Originally, the data mining goal was to develop a deep learning model that could predict seizures based on generated EEG data. This objective quickly changed, as a foundation for deep learning was first needed to get to the point of synthesizing data. Creating this foundation was now the primary objective. Once this was developed, an attempt could be made to generate new EEG data to improve the classification of seizure segments. Unfortunately, the available time did not allow for this, therefore it is recommended for further research. During the process the business objectives became more apparent. Initially, the time-consuming process of assessing EEG data was the motivation for this research. It became clear that medical professionals are not looking to reduce their workload necessarily, but are looking for ways to make their work more efficient by having resources to spare for helping more patients.
Moreover, the reasoning behind the usage of deep learning techniques became more clear. As with many hypes, deep learning was thought to be an interesting approach to try, as it could outperform classical methods. This is of course still the case, but the main reason behind this is different. Due to its performance, there might be indications of seizures that neurologists are unaware of for now. In the next research endeavors, it would make sense to focus on ways to showcase why a model predicted the way it did, to find out which features exist and which are most important. At some point, deep learning results will move from a performance-enteric view to interpretability.
33

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

chunks.source_section_heading: 127 EEG recordings. These are specifically selected for this project by a skilled neurologist based on
chunks.text: 127 EEG recordings. These are specifically selected for this project by a skilled neurologist based on their availability, quality, and relevance to the research. The data consists of various length recordings labeled in the event that seizures occurred. The labeled data allows for a supervised learning strategy. The goal of this research is to develop a deep learning binary classification model capable of detecting anomalies in EEG segments from pediatric comatose patients that are indicative of epileptic seizures. Class imbalance and insufficient data may impose challenges for successful results. Although memory can be virtually increased upon demand, models will not be trained on a GPU, which poses technical limitations. The data science process, preparation and model design produced during the project will serve as a framework for future research in this topic. With this background, the following main research question is proposed: How can deep learning be applied to minimally processed EEG data for the detection of epileptic seizures in pediatric comatose patients? To help provide a substantial conclusion, three sub-questions are formulated. These will allow for a more concrete understanding of how to approach answering the main research question. SQ 1: Which deep learning model architecture is most suitable for identifying the relevant patterns and features in an EEG that are indicative of an epileptic seizure? SQ 2: Which preprocessing techniques should be applied to the raw EEG data to make it trainable for the chosen model? SQ 3: How well does the chosen deep learning model classify EEG segments as seizure or non-seizure? Relevant medical and technical literature are reviewed in Chapter 2, along with the necessary context and business understanding. Chapter 3 outlines the methodology, employing the CRISP-DM framework and ICT Research Methods. The data understanding and preparation is also describe in this chapter. The model and its results are presented and evaluated in Chapter 4. Based on the process and results, a conclusion is provided in Chapter 5. Finally, Chapter 6 touches upon the discussion points with recommendations for further research. 2 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.1 Seizures in comatose patients A seizure - a sudden abnormal electrical activity in the brain - is a common occurrence in comatose patients. Many pediatric patients at Erasmus MC's PICU are in a medically induced comatose state, which is administered in situations where the brain requires a period of rest to recover from trauma or to prevent further damage during medical emergencies. Treating epileptic seizures could be a reason for inducing coma. It is especially difficult to manage and treat subclinical seizures as they may go unnoticed, which could worsen brain injury and hinder the recovery process of the child's developing brain. Careful monitoring of continuous EEG is often needed to detect and observe seizures in comatose patients (Kirkham et al., 2012).

2.2 Electroencephalography EEG is a non-invasive technique used to measure the electrical activity of the brain. The signals originate from neuron activity and are recorded via electrodes placed on a person's scalp. Patients in Erasmus MC's PICU are usually monitored with a 19-electrode set-up (based on head circumference) using the standardized 10-20 system, as shown in Figure 1. Figure 1: Representation of the 10-20 system for EEG Note. Taken fromOverview of EEG, Electrode Placement, and Montages(p.118) by Sazgar and Young, 2019 Using the 10-20 method, technicians calculate the distances between the electrodes to cover all crucial areas; the frontal, temporal, parietal, and occipital lobes. The central part captures activity from the midline of the brain. Measurements are taken between the nasion (bridge of the nose) and inion (back of the head), between the left and right preauricular points (next to the ear), and the head circumference. Marks are made at 10% and 20% distances from the start and end points to place electrodes. Each electrode is represented by a letter related to the brain lobe it is covering. The output is a signal representation of the recordings, created by the voltage (difference in electric potential) between each electrode and a reference electrode. Figure 2 shows the 10-20 electrode positioning along with a 5-channel sample of an EEG recording (a). Neurologists visually assess the signals for seizures and label accordingly (b). This is a time-intensive task, especially when seizures manifest in a subtle way. 3 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige Figure 2: Sample of EEG recording and electrode positioning Note. Taken fromSeizure Detection with Reduced Electroencephalogram Channels: Research Trends and Outlook (p.4) by Maher et al., 2023

2.3 Deep Learning for EEG Analysis The application of data science in healthcare is not a new concept and hospitals such as Erasmus MC conduct extensive research on this subject to hopefully implement techniques on a clinical level in the future. Both machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques are used to achieve data science objectives, but they approach the task in different ways. This is also the case for detecting seizures in EEG data. Machine learning relies on carefully engineered features from the data, which are then used to train models. ML for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection commonly adapts feature extraction methods including time domain, time-frequency domain, frequency domain, fourier transform, discrete wavelet transform, and continuous wavelet transform features (Ahmad et al., 2022). Deep learning techniques, on the other hand, are known for automatically learning features directly from raw or minimally processed EEG data using neural networks. This eliminates the need for feature engineering and may help neurologists to find new indicators of seizure activity in EEG fragments. While DL techniques tend to outperform classical methods, the challenge is that it generally requires larger datasets and more computational resources. More importantly, DL model results tend to be less interpretable compared to the classical ML methods, which makes it difficult to justify its implementation.

2.3.1 Deep Learning Architectures Research shows there are various approaches in applying deep learning for EEG analysis. Insights from literature study can be used to answer the first sub-question:Which deep learning model architecture is most suitable for identifying the relevant patterns and features in an EEG that are indicative of an epileptic seizure? Three reviews have been published that compile results from many articles in which DL is applied for tasks related to EEG data (Craik et al., 2019; Roy et al., 2019; Shoeibi et al., 2021). According to all three, the most common approach in deep learning model architectures for EEG analysis is by far a convolutional neural network (CNN). Although differing in favor across publications, other common architectures tend to be recurrent neural network (RNN), CNN combinations, autoencoder, and deep belief network. For seizure detection tasks the most common architectures are CNN, RNN and CNN-RNN combinations. 4 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.3.2 Convolutional Neural Networks Seizure detection studies show a strong preference for Convolutional neural networks, which is the selected architecture for 45-55% of the reviewed papers by Craik et al. (2019) and Shoeibi et al. (2021). Each layer in a CNN learns to detect different features, which are used as the input to the next layer. It uses shared weights and bias values across all hidden neurons in a layer to detect the same feature (MathWorks, n.d.). "Pooling layers reduce the size of the input pattern to the next convolutional layer. Batch normalization, dropout, early stopping, unsupervised or semi unsupervised learning, and regularization techniques prevent the learned network from overfitting and increase the learning ability and speed" (Shoeibi et al., 2021). After features are extracted in the layers, a final layer for classification outputs probabilities for the classes. In the context of this research, this results in binary classification. Unlike the other two reviews, the research done by Shoeibi et al. (2021) focuses specifically on the detection of epileptic seizures, rather than other EEG analysis or classification tasks. It is mentioned that models with 2D-CNN architectures, followed by 1D-CNN models, are most commonly used for seizure detection. This refers to the dimension of the input data used. Architectures with 2D-CNN layers are mostly used for tasks involving images, which is why in EEG analysis the signals tend to first be converted into an image representation as a spectrogram, as shown in Figure 3. An advantage of transferring the EEG signal to a visual representation of the frequency spectrum over time is that the features are already handed to the model. However, from a neurological perspective, important features can be lost in the transformation to a spectrogram. Research by Ansari et al. (2019) for seizure detection presented a 2D-CNN and a random forest classifier to automatically optimize feature selection and binary classification. The EEG data used originates from Sophia's neonatal ICU (Erasmus MC). 55 features are used in the algorithms, extracted from the frequency domain, time domain, and information theory. Performance metrics of the CNN were: AUC of 88%, recall of 82%, and specificity of 84%. Figure 3: 2D-CNN model pipeline with frequency spectrum Note. Taken fromEpileptic Seizures Detection Using Deep Learning Techniques: A Review(p. 8) by Shoeibi et al., 2021 5 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige In 1D-CNN architectures, it is possible to feed raw or minimally processed data to the network (Figure 4). This method helps to preserve original information from the signal, which could enable the model to learn new relevant indicators of seizures. However, it may be more difficult and need more data for the model to learn these features on its own. It is also possible to make changes to the input shape of a 2D-CNN model to make it process the EEG signals as one-dimensional, as implemented by Gramacki and Gramacki (2022). In this case, the same window setup could be used, but with Conv2D layers instead. This paper focuses on providing a framework for seizure detection using a CNN model, as authors often do not provide information to reproduce their results. Their model obtained 96-97% accuracy results for binary classification with balanced datasets. Figure 4: 1D-CNN model pipeline with windowed EEG signals Note. Taken fromEpileptic Seizures Detection Using Deep Learning Techniques: A Review(p. 12) by Shoeibi et al., 2021

2.3.3 Rationale for Model Architecture CNNs are known for capturing spatial features, which can be important in the case of seizures as they can manifest in specific brain regions. Learning the relationships of signals across different channels can be valuable. When seizures occur, specific spatial patterns differ from normal brain activity, which is exactly what a model should recognize in EEG segments. If temporal features are important in the DL objective, a CNN might not be right choice. They are not able to capture sequences of events as well as other architectures such as RNNs. This is why many papers use RNNs or a CNN-RNN combination if temporal features are of importance. The focus of this model is to classify segments of EEG data, in which temporal features are not as relevant for now. In additional, there is a strong preference for using minimally processed data to explore if deep learning is able to learn patterns from (limited) data on its own. Extracting features does not fit the focus of this project, and transforming the data to a visual representation might lose features that one is not aware of. Based on this and the reviewed literature, the first sub-question can be answered: the right approach for this research is to develop a model using a convolutional neural network architecture. More specifically, a 2D-CNN with one-dimensional input or a 1D-CNN may be used to identify relevant patterns and characteristics in an EEG fragment with epileptic seizures. The intended approach is a supervised learning technique, as the data is likely to be too complex and in short supply for unsupervised learning. However, there are plans for an unsupervised learning approach for EEG analysis at Erasmus MC in the future. For this, extensive data is needed and data pools for sharing across medical institutions are necessary. With unlabeled data (as is not the case in the context of this project), experts are needed to confirm unsupervised model results. 6 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.4 Data A vailability Constraints Most of the studies use a variety of publicly available datasets, including intracranial data and canine EEG data. Only 30% of reviewed papers by Shoeibi et al. (2021) include private clinical data for seizure detection. Sharing patient data introduces ethical and legal constraints. Some collections of largehealthy controldatasets show the problems of access to healthcare, as participants will see this as an opportunity to get a free health check. The lack of available data makes the development of representative models for epileptic seizure difficult, as complete datasets are not shared publicly. They often include only portions of the data and are specifically tailored for certain studies (motor movement, emotion recognition, brain-computer interfaces, etc.). In addition, combining datasets is generally not possible, due to differences in protocols, recording equipment, electrode configurations, and sampling rates.

2.5 EEG Processing While deep learning approaches often leave out the need to perform feature engineering, it may still be needed to preprocess, clean, and augment the data to ensure quality. This step can be demanding and require expertise and knowledge of EEG data. This depends largely on the nature of the data. Before processing, data must first be loaded into the development environment of choice. Depending on the number of subjects and recording time, this can be a time-consuming and memory-intensive process. This varies significantly across studies, as seen in Figure 5 (Roy et al., 2019). Figure 5: Number of subjects and recording time (min) from reviewed papers Note. Taken fromDeep Learning-Based Electroencephalography Analysis: A Systematic Review(p.12) by Roy et al., 2019 Several essential steps are often applied when preprocessing EEG data. The steps applied are largely dependent on the nature of the data and the task at hand, but often includes different types of filtering, independent component analysis (ICA), downsampling, referencing, extracting events or annotations, and epoching (MNE Developers, 2023). 7 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.5.1 Filtering EEG data often contains noise, which can be from a physiological and non-physiological source (Newman, 2020). Physiological sources of noise are generated by movement from muscles, eyes, breathing, provided care and even heartbeats. An example of muscle contraction artifact in shown in Figure 6. This noise causes artifacts that can be cleaned up with ICA. Figure 6: Example of a muscle contraction artifact in EEG data Note: Taken from EEG-ERP Preprocessing - Neural Data Science in Python by Newman, 2020 Artifacts from non-physiological sources include the movement of electrodes and their wires, and high-frequency noise created by electrical interference. In Europe, this creates artifacts at 50 Hz. These are handled with a notch filter, which allows for the suppression of a specific range of frequencies. Because electrical noise often manifests as interference at multiples of the frequency, its harmonics (100 Hz) are also included in filter. Bandpass filters allow a specific range of frequencies to pass through. These filters are applied to the raw data to catch signals that cause aliasing, which represent high-frequency noise as low-frequency artifacts. In that sense they distort the true signal. Newman (2020) mentions "Because of aliasing, the highest frequency that one can accurately record at a given sampling rate is called the Nyquist frequency." As a rule, 1/3 of the sampling rate should be used for low-pass filtering. The high-pass filter has a standard cutoff of 0.1 Hz.

2.5.2 Downsampling Gramacki and Gramacki (2022) downsample the data used in the research, acting as a form of data smoothing. With a sampling frequency of 256 Hz, they decide the frequency is too high and unnecessarily increases the size of the input data. The data is downsampled by a coefficient of 4, resulting in a signal frequency of 64 Hz. This is not a choice all researchers make. Park et al. (2018) used recordings with a sampling rate of 200Hz and 256 Hz, but did not apply downsampling. Downsampling can cause problems if not carefully applied. Seizure patterns may involve specific frequencies that are indications for detection. If a downsampling rate is too low, short seizures may also be missed. 8 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.5.3 Referencing
Setting a reference provides the base point against which the electrical activity of the other electrodes can be measured. The reference electrode is usually placed at a neutral point and can be seen on a montage behind or to the side of the ear (M1, M2 or A1, A2). Such a reference electrode is not always included in data, in which case the average of all channels is taken as a reference point. Re-referencing is also possible when one wants to recompute the data.

2.5.4 Epoching & Extracting Labels Extracting events from data is often done for experimental reasons, such as when working with stimulus responses. In that case, the data is often segmented into epochs related to the events. The resulting epochs length is based on the length of the event. In the case of seizure detection, annotations labels are more common, rather than events. Although, it is also possible to convert annotations to event with MNE-Python. In this context it is more common to create fixed length epochs from the EEG recording, for which events are not needed. The length of an epoch varies across studies, depending on the subject, the data and the task. Some studies also experiment with different epoch lengths to determine which window yields the best results. Like many others, Gramacki and Gramacki (2022) used segments of 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 seconds. It is also possible to create overlapping epochs, more commonly known as a sliding window in data science. This increases the amount of data fed to the model and can be beneficial especially with imbalanced data. However, it is possible that this introduces bias and it is important to make sure there is no data leakage present in the different datasets used. A visual representation is shown in Figure 7. Roy et al. (2019) mentions that there is no clear standard in overlapping approaches. Figure 7: Segmenting of EEG data and terminology Note. 2.6 Model Evaluation The metrics described in this section are relevant for the binary classification task. Evaluation metrics are used in various stages of a modeling process, including during training, validation, testing, and prediction. During the training and validation of a model, metrics are used to assess the performance of the model on the training dataset. Training and validation loss are typically the most important probabilistic metrics for measuring model performance. Accuracy is often monitored for judging model performance as well. It is, however, possible to use multiple metrics and there are a number of other fitting classification metrics based on True (T) /False (F) positives (P) and negatives (N)(Keras, n.d.): Area under the curve (AUC) of the Receiver operating characteristic (ROC), precision, and recall. Each class belonging to a confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN) can also be used for monitoring. These mentioned metrics are also used in the testing and prediction phase of modeling. Additionally, the F1 score is used to evaluate prediction results.

2.6.1 Loss
Loss provides a measure of how well the model is learning the patterns in the training data. It represents the error between the model predictions and the true target values (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.).
Model weights are adjusted during training to minimize the loss value. For binary classification, as is the case for this project, binary cross-entropy is the most commonly used loss function (Ansari et al.,
2019; Gramacki and Gramacki, 2022; Park et al., 2018). This returns a probability score between 0 and
1, for example, when using a sigmoid activation function. Monitoring the loss function is important for noticing overfitting and underfitting of the model.

2.6.2 Confusion Matrix
A confusion matrix allows for a visual representation of predictions and actual cases: true positive, true negative, false positive, and false negative. A positive case refer to a seizure segment, whereas a negative case is a non-seizure segment. Ideally, one would prefer a high number of TP and TN, as this would indicate that the model was able to predict the classes well (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.).

2.6.3 Accuracy
Accuracy is the ratio of the sum of TP and TN to the total number of instances, where 1 equals perfect predictions. It calculates how often predictions match the true labels and is a popular metric used for classification (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.). Many researches publish their results using accuracy as a direct measure of success. It is important to note that using accuracy as the main metric with class imbalance does not give an accurate representation of the results. Depending on the context, other metrics might be more valuable.
Accuracy = T P + T N
T P + T N + F P + F N
10

Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

2.6.4 ROC-AUC
The ROC curve is a graphical representation of the trade-off between the TP rate and TN rate. The
AUC quantifies the model performance by measuring the area under the ROC curve, ranging from 0 to
1. Again, a higher value means a better prediction (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.). Random chance, meaning that the model was no able to discriminate between classes, is close to 0.5. ROC-AUC is often used in clinical context, especially when class distribution is imbalanced.

2.6.5 Precision
Precision measures the accuracy of positive predictions, where the output is the ratio of TP to the sum of TP and FP (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.). It is an important metric when there is a need for avoiding false positives. In the case of clinical implementation, a high number of false positives could mean unnecessary interventions. A high precision means that all positive predictions made by a model are correct, while a lower precision suggests that there are false positives.
P recision = T P
T P + F P

2.6.6 Recall
Recall is focused on the ability to correctly identify positive classes out of all actual positives, as the ratio of TP to the sum of TP and FB (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.). In medical context it is especially important to make sure that positive cases are identified. A low recall value would mean that there is a higher number of false negatives.
Recall = T P
T P + F N

2.6.7 F1-score
As can be understood from precision and recall, a balance between the two is important. The F1 score specifies this balance between the two metrics. Again, ranging from 0 to 1, where 1 represents perfect precision and recall balance (Scikit-learn developers, n.d.). It is useful as there is often a priority in either recall or precision, when having to choose between these two. A low number of false positives and false negatives is preferable, but not always attainable. When one of the two introduce life-threatening scenarios, a target balance should be set.
F1 = 2 × P recision × Recall
P recision + Recall

3.4 Research Design
This research design serves as the overarching framework guiding the approach to answering the sub-questions and main research question. A quantitative research design is adopted, given the nature of the research objective - to develop a DL classification model for detecting epileptic seizures in EEG
fragments of pediatric comatose patients. This type of design is deemed suitable as the results of the study present findings from statistical measurements, where patterns in EEG data are analyzed by the model in question. Before the model is developed, the data is analyzed and prepared.

3.5.1 Data Collection Eligibility Patients from infancy to adolescence admitted to a Pediatric Intensive Care Unit and monitored by EEG are the primary focus of this research. Only patients in a comatose state are included in this focus. Patients may be medically induced for a number of unspecified reasons, such as traumatic brain injury, cardiac arrest, and to treat prolonged or severe seizures. There are a total of 127 EEG recordings from unique subjects available, each with a distinct duration that sums up to nearly 138 days of continuous data. The average recording duration per subject is 26 hours. Data Access Access to EEG data from these patients is provided within the research setting of Erasmus MC. The Neurology department facilitates the collection of relevant and specific data originating from Sophia's PICU. The recording equipment used is the OSG BrainRT system, according to the 10-20 system with 19 electrodes and a sampling frequency of 256 Hz. To further use the data for analysis and modeling it is made available in EDF format, stored on and accessible through a digital research environment. A virtual machine on the workspace provides access to a dedicated project directory where the data is stored. This is an isolated workspace without Internet access to minimize the risk of a data breach. To protect privacy of patients, the subject identifiers have been replaced with pseudonyms before storing on the workspace. As patient data is confidential, the data is not shared as a part of this report but providing statistical descriptions and plotted segments is approved. Loading The data is loaded with MNE-Python, a package for exploring, visualizing, and analyzing human neurophysiological data, including EEG. Loading the data can be very memory intensive. The EDF file size ranges from 7 MB to 9 GB, but loading the data requires much more working memory than its file size. The values are represented as floating-point numbers with 6 decimal places. The virtual machine provides 64 GB RAM with 16 cores. To limit memory constraints, several solutions are applied. First of all, the data is not preloaded, but read only initially. This is done for each file individually, as the memory available does not allow for all the data to be loaded at the same time. During the reading, channel types are inferred, allowing the selection of only EEG channels. This omits technical and other physiological data channels that are not relevant for analysis. 15 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige Recordings that still exceed the memory available are approached differently and split into two processes, which is described in the Data Preparation phase. It is important to still include these large files, as more seizures are generally captured in longer recordings and such data is often in short supply. The data contains annotations that serve as labels for the model input. The annotations indicate occurrences, such as artifacts from movement or provided care, abnormal EEG patterns, and seizures marked with starting and ending times. The only relevant annotations for this research are those of seizures, hence why these are only selected during further analysis.

3.5.4 Data Quality The properties of the data are consistent across subjects. There is only one subject (M6b_0000513#01) from the data selection that differs in number of electrodes, sampling frequency, and lowpass. Normally this is 19 electrodes, 256 Hz and 128 Hz, respectively. The one exception misses the Pz electrode and has a sampling frequency 250 Hz, along with a lowpass of 125 Hz. This empty column is filled with the mean value of all other electrodes, as consulted with the supervising neurologist. The data contains artifacts caused by electrical interference at 50 Hz and its harmonics. To suppress the noise, bandpass filters can be applied in the data preparation phase. These artifacts are detected by applying a Fast Fourier Transform. The power spectral density plot of the sample subjects shows spikes at 50 and 100 Hz in the EEG. There are other recordings that also include a spike at around 10 Hz, as seen in Figure 11. Figure 11: Power spectral density plot According to Newman, 2020, this is common spike in the alpha band, associated with a state of relaxed wakefulness. The lowpass filter does not allow for frequencies above 128 Hz. If that would not be the case, there would also be a visible spike at the third harmonic of 50 Hz, which is 150 Hz. The power is highest at the lowest frequencies (between 0 and 20 Hz) and decreases with higher frequencies. This decrease is known as the 1/f property or flicker noise, which is also commonly observed in EEG. Other noise or artifacts in the data are not further analyzed and cleaned due to the comatose state of the patient, as verified by the supervising neurologist. It is simply the case that a comatose patient will not move enough to cause such excessive noise. There is also no other missing or impaired data. None of the recordings was analyzed forbad channels. This refers to channels that are considered unreliable, due to artifacts or anything else that makes them less suitable for analysis. This choice is made to keep the data as raw as possible, to retain clinical practice. 18 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

3.6.1 Selection A case selection is applied, where only EEG recordings containing one or more seizures are eligible for further research. This might provide the DL model more targeted information about the distinctive patterns associated with epileptic seizures, rather than also including data from patients that did not contain any seizures during the recording. The limitation of this is that the model is not inclusive of patients that have no seizures. It might not be able to accurately detect and classify segments of recordings that are completely free of seizures. The selection reduces the available data to 37 EEG recordings that include seizures, which now consists of a total of nearly 78 days of continuous data available, of which 3 days account for seizures. As the loading process states in Chapter 3.5.1, only EEG data is selected to limit memory constraints while loading data. This is considered a necessary preprocessing step. Despite this modification, there are still 6 out of the 37 selected files that cannot be fully loaded due to its considerable size. Since they contain many seizures, the six recordings are handled differently to still include them in the selection.

3.6.2 Problem Handling for Large Files The six long recordings that cannot be fully loaded are handled in segments. These large files are cropped by its seizure annotations, resulting in a Raw object for each individual seizure. These are loaded and concatenated at a later point. The same amount on non-seizure data from this subject is then loaded in segments as well. This selection is in line with the data mining goals, regardless of the technical limitations. The downside of this approach is that, due to fixed length epoching done at a later point, this means that short seizures with a duration of less than 10 seconds are completely excluded. 3.6.3 Cleaning Before the data is loaded and cleaned, it is first read with necessary preprocessing steps, such as inferring channel types and only selecting EEG channels. While all the files are read individually, files that contain no annotations with the description indicative of a seizure, they are skipped. After this, each file can be loaded and undergo steps that are considered cleaning. Filtering The electrical noise creating artifacts is removed with a notch filter, as described in Chapter 3.5.4. It selects the frequencies 50 Hz and 100 Hz. Other harmonics are not necessarily to select, since the lowpass filter rejects frequencies above 128 Hz. Besides the notch filter, a bandpass filter is applied. The high-pass filter uses a standard cutoff of 0.1 Hz and the low-pass filter is 70 Hz as a given, based on the Nyquist frequency rule. Referencing After the data is loaded, referencing is applied to provide a base point for the electrodes. Since the data does not contain a reference electrode, the average of all channels is used as a reference point to measure electrical activity against. Missing Data While the data is continuous and there is no missing data, one of the subjects (M6b_0000513#01) does not include the Pz electrode. This column is added and filled with the mean value of the other electrodes for each data point. This recording also has a sampling frequency of 250 Hz instead of 256 Hz. To remain consistent across each EEG, it is resampled to 256 Hz. This creates 6 interpolated data points for every recorded second.

3.6.4 Constructing
Seizure Extraction
The seizure labels are stored in Raw's annotations. They can be found using its description. The annotations are converted to a DataFrame, which results in the onset and duration and in seconds for each seizure per recording. By summing these, the end time of the seizure is calculated and added to the DataFrame.
Epoching
As the data mining goal is to classify EEG segments, fixed length epochs are created from the recording.
After consulting, it was decided that each epoch will be 10 seconds long with no overlap. The epochs are converted to a DataFrame, and the original time in seconds is restored.

3.6.5 Integrating
Merging
The epochs DataFrame and seizure annotations are merged on the count of the recording and the onset of each seizure. A column is added to add binary labels to, indicating each row as seizure (1) or non-seizure (0). Each row at closest time match between each seizure start and end time are marked as 1. With the memory constraints it is not yet possible to merge the data of all the recordings into one DataFrame.

3.6.6 Formatting Rounding As mentioned, all values are presented as floats with 6 decimal places. This precision is not needed and rounding all values to 2 decimal places will save memory and be more computationally efficient. Reshaping The data mining goals state that segments should be classified as seizure or non-seizure. The epochs are created, but the DataFrame still presents each data point as an individual row. Reshaping takes place so that each record represents an entire epoch of 10 seconds. This is done by grouping and aggregating float values and putting them an array. The target column can now present an entire epoch as seizure/non-seizure. Its value is set to the (first) most common value. This means that, for example, an epoch with 6 seconds of seizure data will be considered a seizure segment. On the other hand, an epoch with 4 seconds of seizure data will be considered a non-seizure segment. This approach is approved from the expert perspective. Appointing seizure labels to epochs that consist of only seizure data is also possible. There is no right of wrong approach, but it should be a conscious design choice. This decision is made to not further reduce the occurrence of seizure epochs. In comparison, Gramacki and Gramacki (2022) preferred not falling into a seizure or non-seizure area within the same epoch. It should be mentioned that, besides the excluded seizures as described in Chapter 3.6.2, more seizures are excluded because of this. Luckily, seizures shorter than 5 seconds do not occur often in the data. Table 6 supports this. One of the recordings (M15_0000615#01) only contains one seizure that lasts 3 seconds. The seizure is too short to be classified as a seizure epoch, and this leaves no other seizures in this recording. Because of this, the subject is no longer included in the data selection. This reduces the number of subjects selected for further analysis to 36. Class balancing Every recording contains more non-seizure epochs than seizure epochs. To avoid class imbalance, the DataFrame is separated based on the target value. The number of seizure epochs per recording is calculated, after which the same number of non-seizure epochs are selected randomly. These two subsets are concatenated, presenting the final DataFrame per recording. Exporting After extracting the subject ID for later identification, each DataFrame is exported to a feather file. This format is chosen for its high read and write performance, and ability to store complex data types efficiently. 21 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

4.1 Modeling Technique The chosen modeling technique is a convolutional neural network, as described in Chapter 2.3.3. Since it is possible to either design a network with 1D-CNN layers or with 2D-CNN layers, both of these types are used in different models. Such a network combines convolutional, pooling, and dense layers. When using 2D-CNN layers, a three-dimensional input shape is expected. In order for this to work, the channel channel size is set to 1, as the data is two-dimensional (data points across time & electrodes). Several other assumptions are made when it comes to using a CNN. Spatial patterns are expected to exist in the data. The patterns are local and can move across the data, but there should be some consistency in features across the data. Another assumption is that there should be a large amount of data available to train the model with, although there is also no strict threshold for the right amount of data. There should be little to no class imbalance for reliable results, and as mentioned before, the input data should match the 2 or 3 dimensional structure depending on the layer. The shape needs to be consistent, which is why each recording should have the same number of electrodes and sampling frequency once it is fed to the model. Any missing data should be addressed. The data used and processed in the Data Preparation phase seems to meet these expectations. However, it is not clear whether the patterns in the data meet the criteria, which is necessary for the model to identify the indications of a seizure without applying feature engineering.

4.2 Test Design Prior to building the model, the data set is separated into subsets. All the data is combined before performing the data split. The steps in the Data Preparation phase presented in the Methodology Chapter (3.6) resulted in a feather file for each of the 36 subjects with a balanced amount of seizure and non-seizure epochs. To use this data for modeling, each file is first read with Pandas and transformed to a DataFrame. It is then combined to form a large DataFrame that contains all the data by concatenating it. There are 51.194 epochs, and with 19 electrodes this is a total of nearly 2.5 billion EEG data points. The dataset is then separated into variablesX and y, representing the features and target variable as model input, in respective order. The input features include 19 EEG channels with minimally preprocessed amplitude float values. The target variable is the predicting value for each epoch as 0 (non-seizure) or 1 (seizure). The model is trained to learn patterns in theX features that are associated with y labels. The features are in a nested structure, with each value being a NumPy array with 2560 floats, fitting the 10 second segments and sampling frequency of 256 Hz. When separating the data,X turns into a multidimensional array as there are 19 EEG channels. To feed the data into the model, the nested structure is flattened into a one-dimensional list. The data is further split into shuffled and random train-test-validation subsets, with 70% being used for training, and the remaining 30% equally divided between test and validation sets. Lastly, the input features for each subset are reshaped to a three-dimensional structure (number of samples, 2560, 19). This is done to align the data with the expected input format of the model, where each sample is a 2D array of size (2560, 19). 22 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

4.3 Model Design A summary of the 1D-CNN and 2D-CNN model architecture can be found in Table 2 and 3. Both models have three convolutional layers designed to learn features from the input data. The model takes input with a shape of (2560, 19) in the 1D-CNN and (2560, 19, 1) in the 2D-CNN. The 2D layer normally expects a 3D input tensor, which is why the channel is defined as 1. Each of the convolutional layers use the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function. This is a standard choice as one of the most commonly used activation functions. After each convolutional layer, there is a MaxPooling layer to reduce the spatial dimensions of the representation. This helps to reduce computational complexity and improve translation invariance. This is followed by a Flatten layer to flatten the 2D spatial output into 1D vectors. This prepares the data for the fully connected (Dense) layers. The first Dense layer has 128 neurons and uses ReLU activation. This is the hidden layer, responsible for learning complex patterns in the flattened data. The final layer (Dense) has 2 neurons for this binary classification task using sigmoid activation. The sigmoid function squashes its input values to the range of 0,1. The output is presented as the probability of the input belonging to the positive class (seizure). The architecture has an increasing pattern in convolutional filters, starting at 32 and ending at 128 kernels. The model starts by capturing simple and low-level features, and ends with capturing complex and abstract features in the final layer. Starting with a smaller number of filters in the first layer is a common design approach in this context and makes the model more computationally efficient, which is an important factor in this case as the model is not trained on GPU. Starting with a simpler representation of the data also helps to prevent overfitting, especially with limited training data. 4.4 Model Training Adam is the optimization algorithm chosen for the training of the model. This is a standard approach, and its parameters have not been tweaked. Binary cross-entropy is the chosen loss function, given that the model concerns binary classification and a sigmoid activation function. Besides the loss, multiple metrics are selected to evaluate the model's performance during training and validation. These are Binary Accuracy, AUC, Precision, and Recall. The selected optimizer, loss function, and metrics are chosen based on the characteristics of the data and the classification task. To prevent overfitting, improve model generalization and use resources efficiently, EarlyStopping is used as a regularization technique. The model's performance on the validation set during training is monitored to stop the training process when the performance does not improve or starts to degrade. The patience is set to 10 iterations and with restoring of best model weights. This value is selected after analyzing the learning curves from a 1D-CNN that was tested with 100 iterations and no EarlyStopping (Figure 17). A strong case of overfitting occurs as the validation loss starts heavily increasing past epoch 10, while the training loss remains quite steady. When the training accuracy increases with experience, the validation accuracy does not follow this trend. This highlights the need for using EarlyStopping during training to minimize overfitting. The metric monitored in EarlyStopping is the validation loss. A few tests were done using other metrics (validation accuracy and f1-score), which did not improve the results significantly and only increased the loss. The models are trained with a batch size of 32, which is the default value. 24 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige
