5 Conclusion This research focused on the development of a deep neural network for the classification of epileptic seizures in EEG fragments. The process involved understanding the medical context and the EEG data, preparing the data for modeling after considering modeling techniques, creating a test and model design, performing modeling, and evaluating its results. Three sub-questions were formulated to navigate and provide an answer to the following main question: How can deep learning be applied to minimally processed EEG data for the detection of epileptic seizures in pediatric comatose patients? 5.1 Sub-question 1 SQ 1: Which deep learning model architecture is most suitable for identifying the relevant patterns and features in an EEG that are indicative of an epileptic seizure? While there are plenty of valid options of deep learning architectures for this task, the literature review proved a strong preference for the usage of 2D and 1D convolutional neural networks for the detection of seizures in an EEG. Considering the preference of using minimally processed EEG data - as medical experts are interested in learning fromraw data patterns - this is a fitting approach. To answer this sub-question more specifically, both 2D layers with one-dimensional input and 1D layers can be used in the CNN design. The theoretical framework shows that 1D convolutional layers are typically used in combination with minimally processed EEG data. Although data is generally transformed to a visual representation of the EEG when using 2D convolutional layers, it is also possible to leave out this process by forcing the CNN to observe one dimension in its channel size. This prevents the possible loss of information when converting to a spectrogram. Until more seizure data is available, supervised learning remains the right approach. Once temporal aspects become more relevant to include in model results, a recurrent neural network or CNN-RNN combination may be more appropriate for further research. This could be the case when there is an interest in analyzing an EEG recording in its entirety, instead of separate fragments as was the case in this research. 28 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige 5.2 Sub-question 2 SQ 2: Which preprocessing techniques should be applied to the raw EEG data to make it trainable for the chosen model? The preprocessing techniques applied to the data are dependent on the characteristics of the data, the task at hand and the chosen model technique. In the context of this research it was desirable to keep the data as raw as possible, since medical professionals also have to work with the rough and noisy data in the field. This is why limited techniques are applied to clean, construct and format the data. Artifacts caused by electrical interference are always removed and a bandpass filter is applied to clean the data. Other artifacts, such as from nursing or other movement could still exist in the data but are not cleaned. There is less need for doing this as the data concerns comatose patients. A reference point needs to be provided to serve as a base point for the electrodes. In this research, missing data occurred once and was handled by imputing with the mean values of other electrodes. This was done because the model assumes consistent dimension across segments, but in practice physicians will have to do with the electrodes available. Seeing that the task at hand was to classify segments of an EEG, the recordings were epoched with a fixed length of ten seconds and reshaped by grouping and aggregating channels values. For the model to classify a whole segment as seizure or non-seizure, each epoch was labeled based on the most common value. To avoid class imbalance, an equal number of seizure and non-seizure epochs were selected for each recording. 5.3 Sub-question 3 SQ 3: How well does the chosen deep learning model classify EEG segments as seizure on non-seizure? The results presented two types of convolutions neural networks: one with 1D layers and the other with 2D layers. These models performed similarly on the classification and its related metrics, although differences were observed in the learning curves and predicted probabilities. The CNN with 1D layers showed a more stable loss curve over epochs with less of a gap between the training and validation set, compared to the CNN with 2D layers. In terms of accuracy over epochs, the 2D-CNN model showed a more steady curve. A model with 2D layers shows more confidence in probabilities close to 0 and 1, as the model with 1D layers predicted more segments around the probability threshold (0.5). This was also observed from the AUC-ROC curve. The 1D-CNN showed a preference for classifying segments as seizure, with a higher number of correct as well as false classifications compared to the 2D-CNN. On the other hand, this was the opposite case for the 2D-CNN, which showed a preference for classifying segments as non-seizure. It correctly classified more non-seizure segments, but this also resulted in more false non-seizure segments compared to the results from the 1D-CNN. The performance metrics reflect these results, as can be seen from the precision and recall score. 1D-CNN performed better with a recall score of 0.83 compared to 0.81. On the contrary, 2D-CNN has a better precision score of 0.73 compared to 1D-CNN's 0.71. There is a 0.2 difference in these performance metrics. With this trade-off, both F1 scores are 0.77. The overall accuracy of the 2D-CNN was slightly better with 0.76, compared to 0.75. 29 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

5.4 Main question In evaluation of these results, it is important to link back to the business objectives and data mining goals. These also relate to the main research question. The business objectives and its corresponding success criteria were defined as: A healthy population and excellent care through research and education Improve PICU bed occupancy and nurse staffing Discover new biomarkers for seizures in EEG The success criteria for these objectives was not based on direct achievement. Instead, the results and development of the model contribute by setting a foundation for further research. This takes us back to the data mining goals: Develop a functional deep learning classification model that can distinguish between EEG fragment with seizure activity and non-seizure brain activity This is determined to be successful if: The model performs better than a random classifier with at least 60% accuracy on a balanced dataset Prioritizes recall, with an F1 score between 0.7 and 0.8 Both models achieved an accuracy of at least 75% and an F1 score of 0.77. Although the results were very similar, 1D-CNN performed the best according to the success criteria set, since it has a higher recall score. From a medical perspective, minimizing the risk of missing seizures is most important. In conclusion, this research focused on developing a deep learning model for the classification of seizure and non-seizure EEG segments in comatose pediatric patients. The data mining goals, along with Erasmus MC's objective to improve patient care through research, are achieved. The sub-questions reveal the steps that should be taken to preprocess the data in a way to minimally modifies the data without affecting its true representation. This is done by filtering with a high-pass of 0.1 Hz, a low-pass of 70 Hz and a notch filter that select the frequencies of 50 Hz and Hz to remove electrical noise. Referencing should be applied and missing data in the form of left out channels should be addressed. In accordance with the data mining success criteria and the sub-question answers, the most suitable deep learning technique for this minimally processed EEG data of pediatric comatose patients is determined to be a convolutional neural network with 1D layers. Looking forward, this research lays the groundwork for further research and innovation at Erasmus MC. The implementation of a highly accurate model could potentially address the shortage of nurses in the PICU, and ongoing research may uncover novel biomarkers for seizure detection. 30 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

6.1 Interpretation of Results The performance metrics achieve the objectives, but the results are not excellent. For the purpose of the research this is fine, as the models are not expected to be implemented but serve as groundwork for further research. None of the models trained made it past epoch 15 when using EarlyStopping. The learning curves accompany this, as they show strong signs of overfitting past epoch 15 or earlier. In comparison, a reviewed article that was used as a reference (Gramacki and Gramacki, 2022) trained models with 300 epochs. With complex data, it was assumed that the developed models would show signs of underfitting instead. The distribution of predictions as probabilities are not shared in the literature that was reviewed, which does not allow for comparison. In the results of this research, a lot of predictions has probabilities around 0.5, which would suggest that the model is not extremely confident about the classification. The research states that recall is prioritized from a medical perspective, in order to not risk missing seizures. However, the one of the business objectives mentions the improvement of nurse staffing. A point could be made from a nursing perspective that precision is equally important to recall, if not more. False alarms for seizure detection might only increase the workload of the medical staff. It is a trade-off that must be made and the stakeholders are expected to have different priorities. Another interesting point of view is that of the (possibly) unknown indicators of seizures. If such indicators in EEG data truly exist and can be learned using deep learning, evaluation through performance metrics and a confusion matrix might not make sense. After all, some segments may include indications of seizures that a neurologist was not able to identify. This could technically result in reported false positives and negatives, without these truly being false. It should be mentioned that the results of this research are certainly not expected to have captured undetected seizures, but this could be an interesting thought for future research. This is why adequate techniques and visualization techniques will be necessary to implement to interpret the black-box results of a deep learning model. A way to interpret the results from this research is that, due to using a validation set, the size of the training set is not large enough. This causes overfitting and limits the ability to correctly classify. It could also be the case that one of the recordings with many epochs contributes much to the model weights, but is not representative of the other patients. This could introduce bias to the model. The results may still be acceptable, but only because the test design includes epochs from patients with a lot of seizure data across each subset. This would mean that the model does not generalize well.

6.2 Generalizability While a validation set was used in the test design, it is important to note that no specific cross-validation technique was applied. The data splitting process involved a random separation of the data into subsets for training, validation, and testing. The single split was done without further stratification or grouping. This decision was made due to the available epoch per subject and the dataset size. When training a 2D-CNN with no validation set and only train-test sets, it scored better on accuracy and precision compared to the model that used validation data during the training process. However, recall worsened. This could be explained by the size of the dataset. It is possible that using a portion of the data for validation resulted in insufficient data for training and improving its accuracy. 31 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige Using a cross-validation strategy for splitting the data ensures robustness and avoids data leakage. In a medical context using a subject-wise split is a standard, as otherwise the model might learn patterns in epochs from specific recordings from the training and validation set and will apply the corresponding weights to the test set. This could introduce a form of data leakage. Ensuring there is no overlap in patients across subsets can be done with GroupKFold cross-validation. However, the data used in this research has an imbalanced number of seizure epochs across patients. To solve this imbalance, stratification needs to be applied in combination with nonoverlapping groups through StratifiedGroupKFold. This means that the size of the dataset could be excessively reduced, since every patient will need an equal amount of epochs. Another option could be to apply the LeaveOneGroupOut cross-validation technique. During each model iteration, one patient's data would be used for testing, while the data from all other patients is used for training. Considering that seizure characteristics are patient-specific, using the technique might help the model to capture that, while ensuring that it can generalize across patients. Again, the impact of varying epoch counts is that patients with more recordings contribute more data to the training process.

6.3 Recommendations Cross-validation Those conducting further research are advised to consider the impact of cross-validation on generalizability. Many studies use record-wise cross-validation, which does not ensure that samples from the same patient are not present in both training and test folds. This can overestimate the performance of a classifier. According to Saeb et al. (2016) and Tougui et al. (2021), the only right form of cross-validation in this context is subject-wise, with StratifiedGroupKFold and LeaveOneGroupOut being the only valid techniques. At the same time, researchers should acknowledge that successful implementation of this will depend on the balance of classes and samples. In order to stratify these and obtain satisfactory model results, it might be necessary to implement data augmentation techniques that increase the number of seizure epochs in patients or to increase the amount of seizure data overall. Data Augmentation As mentioned in the theoretical framework, data augmentation steps can be taken to increase the available data. This needs to be approached carefully to avoid bias. Using sliding windows and downsampling non-seizure segments can help address a small dataset. If a large number of epochs need to be generated, overlapping windows do not introduce new information. Instead, it could be more useful to develop a model that can generate fragments on its own. Synthetic Data As mentioned, an interesting avenue of research could be to use modeling techniques to generate synthetic EEG data that simulates seizures. Rasheed et al., 2021 proposed a deep convolutional generative adversarial network to generate synthetic EEG data based on other recordings before detecting seizures using a CNN model. Being able to use synthetic EEG data for modeling would significantly reduce the shortage and the time-consuming process which is acquiring EEG signals. Additionally, this would make predictions easier to make when privacy concerns are less presented. 32 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige

6.4 Process Review Working with the CRISP-DM methodology resulted in multiple iterations of phases in this project. Originally, the data mining goal was to develop a deep learning model that could predict seizures based on generated EEG data. This objective quickly changed, as a foundation for deep learning was first needed to get to the point of synthesizing data. Creating this foundation was now the primary objective. Once this was developed, an attempt could be made to generate new EEG data to improve the classification of seizure segments. Unfortunately, the available time did not allow for this, therefore it is recommended for further research. During the process the business objectives became more apparent. Initially, the time-consuming process of assessing EEG data was the motivation for this research. It became clear that medical professionals are not looking to reduce their workload necessarily, but are looking for ways to make their work more efficient by having resources to spare for helping more patients. Moreover, the reasoning behind the usage of deep learning techniques became more clear. As with many hypes, deep learning was thought to be an interesting approach to try, as it could outperform classical methods. This is of course still the case, but the main reason behind this is different. Due to its performance, there might be indications of seizures that neurologists are unaware of for now. In the next research endeavors, it would make sense to focus on ways to showcase why a model predicted the way it did, to find out which features exist and which are most important. At some point, deep learning results will move from a performance-enteric view to interpretability. 33 Graduation Thesis Angélique Huige
